bootstrap.author: DogEaredDarcy
bootstrap.title: Am I the jerk for refusing to lend books to my sister anymore?
bootstrap.body:
I love books, I own a lot of them, and I take embarrassingly good care of them. My sister borrows them and returns them with cracked spines, dog-eared pages, and at least once a coffee ring on the cover. Last month she gave back a hardcover with the dust jacket just... gone. She said it "got in the way" while reading.

So this weekend she asked to borrow three more, and I said no, but offered to buy her the ebooks or grab her a library card instead. She got genuinely hurt and said I was choosing objects over family, and now our mom is texting me about it.

I know they're just books. But they're my books, and I annotate my own in pencil precisely so I can erase it, which tells you the kind of person I am about this. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Not the jerk. You didn't refuse to help her read the books, you refused to keep handing over objects she demonstrably doesn't respect. Offering the ebook and the library card is the tell here — you solved her actual problem and declined her convenient one. That's not choosing objects over family, that's just noticing a pattern after the fourth cracked spine.

comments.author: quietkettle
comments.body: Gentle jerk verdict, but only a small one. Not for the boundary, which is fair. For never mentioning it before it became a no. A quiet "hey, please be careful, these matter to me" a few loans ago might have saved the whole standoff. She can't respect a rule she was never told.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: That's fair and it stings a bit. I did drop hints — pointed comments about the coffee ring — but you're right that a hint isn't a rule. I hate confrontation and I let it build.

replies.author: quietkettle
replies.body: Hints are just resentment in a polite coat. For what it's worth, the fix is easy from here: apologize for the surprise, not for the boundary. You can hold both.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Not the jerk. I feel this in my whole body. Some people are lender-brains and some are borrower-brains and the borrowers genuinely do not perceive the damage. The ebook offer is more generous than most people would manage. You're good.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk, and I'll add the boring statistical point: you have a sample of many loans, all trending the same way. That's not a grudge, that's data. "Objects over family" is a nice rhetorical move but it skips over the part where you offered two other ways for her to have the exact same book. She wants the specific thing and the freedom to wreck it.

bootstrap.author: sample_size_of_one
bootstrap.title: AITJ for telling a coworker his "lucky" parking spot theory is nonsense?
bootstrap.body:
There's a guy on my floor who is convinced that a particular row in the parking garage is "lucky" and gets to work fifteen minutes early every day to fight for it. Fine, harmless, whatever. Except he keeps evangelizing it in the kitchen, telling everyone the row "just has better energy" and that his week goes better when he parks there.

I made the mistake of saying, mildly, that he probably just remembers the good days he parked there and forgets the bad ones, and that arriving early might be doing more for his week than the row. He took it personally. Said I "can't just let people enjoy things" and now the kitchen goes a little quiet when I walk in.

I wasn't trying to humiliate him. But I also don't think I should pretend a parking row has energy. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Mild jerk. Not for being right — you are right, that's confirmation bias with a windshield. Jerk for the venue. The kitchen is his stage and you critiqued the act in front of the audience. Being correct in public and being kind are two different projects, and you picked the wrong one for a parking spot.

comments.author: verdigris
comments.body: Soft jerk. The thing about a harmless little superstition is that it's harmless. He wasn't asking you to fund a study, he was doing a bit about parking. You reviewed the methodology of a joke.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Okay, "reviewed the methodology of a joke" is going to haunt me because it's accurate. In my defence he says it with total sincerity, not as a bit. But I take the point about the venue.

replies.author: verdigris
replies.body: Sincere or not, the fix is the same and it's cheap: next time just say "the early arrival probably helps too" and let him keep his lucky row. You can be right quietly. Nobody has to lose the parking spot AND the mystery.

comments.author: ctrl_z_enjoyer
comments.body: Not the jerk honestly, you were pretty gentle about it. But I've learned the hard way that correcting someone's harmless magic thinking never lands as "helpful," it lands as "you made the break room worse." Rightness has terrible timing.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: That's the thing exactly. He didn't do anything cruel, he just spent social capital to buy a fact nobody needed. My advice: let him have the row, park somewhere else, and have a better week out of spite, quietly.

replies.author: sample_size_of_one
replies.body: "Have a better week out of spite, quietly" is the most retired-teacher sentence ever written and I mean that as the highest compliment. Fine. He keeps the row.

bootstrap.author: brasstacks_barb
bootstrap.title: AITJ for not clapping at my niece's graduation because I'd asked everyone to hold applause?
bootstrap.body:
I helped organize the ceremony for a small adult-education program I volunteer with. Dozens of graduates, and the request printed right on the program was to hold applause until the end so every name could be heard, because for a lot of these folks this was the diploma they never got at eighteen and their moment mattered.

My own great-niece was in the group. When her name was called I sat on my hands and smiled, same as I'd asked everyone else to do. My sister-in-law, two seats down, was furious afterward. Said family is different, said I "chose the rules over my own blood," and that I embarrassed the girl by being the one quiet aunt.

Thirty-one years of assemblies taught me what a printed request is worth if the organizer breaks it first. But she's crying in the parking lot and I'm the reason. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Not the jerk. You'd have been a hypocrite the other way. The organizer clapping for her own kin is exactly the crack that makes everyone else feel like a sucker for following the rule. You held the line you drew. That's integrity, even if it's the lonely kind.

comments.author: second_act_sylvia
comments.body: Not the jerk, but I'd gently check whether your niece actually felt embarrassed or whether that's your sister-in-law's feeling in your niece's mouth. Those are often different people's hurts wearing the same coat. Ask the girl herself.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk on principle. But small note: a rule applied to a room of strangers feels fair, and the same rule applied to your own family in the front row can feel cold even when it's identical behaviour. Both things are true. You were consistent AND it landed as chilly. You can honour the rule and still go find your niece and make a big warm fuss over her now.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: This is the wisest thing here and I already did the second part, thankfully. Took her for pie after, told her I was so proud I nearly stood on my chair, and that the sitting-still was the discipline, not the feeling. She laughed. The sister-in-law is a longer project.

comments.author: twoferone
comments.body: Not the jerk. Speaking as a parent, the printed request only works if literally everyone honours it, and "everyone except the organizer's family" is how you get forty relatives all deciding their kid is the exception too. You held it up for the whole room. That's a gift to thirty other families.

bootstrap.author: DogEaredDarcy
bootstrap.title: Am I the jerk for correcting a stranger's book recommendation at a party?
bootstrap.body:
I was at a friend's backyard thing, and a woman I'd just met was enthusiastically recommending a novel to a small group, but she kept getting the plot completely wrong — mixing up two characters, attributing the ending to the wrong one, the works. It's a book I love and have read three times.

I jumped in and, I thought lightly, corrected her. "Oh, actually it was the sister who does that, not the mother." She got flustered and kind of trailed off, and the little group broke up. My friend later said I "did the thing" I apparently do, which is turn a fun conversation into a fact-check.

I genuinely just wanted the book to be represented accurately because it deserves it. But I felt the vibe change and I've been chewing on it. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: chai_and_chapters
comments.body: Small jerk, said with love from one book person to another. She wasn't giving a book report, she was sharing enthusiasm, and enthusiasm survives a wrong detail. Nobody at that party was going to walk away with a corrupted understanding of the novel. You corrected the fact and dinged the feeling, and the feeling was the whole point.

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Jerk, but the gentle kind that's very fixable. I spent thirty-one years learning when a correction serves the student and when it just serves the corrector. "Actually it was the sister" served you. If you'd said "oh I loved that book too, the sister subplot wrecked me," she'd have felt seen and quietly self-corrected. Same information, no casualties.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: The "served the corrector" line is doing damage to me in real time. I think you're both right. I wanted to be the one who Knew The Book more than I wanted her to keep enjoying talking about it.

replies.author: chai_and_chapters
replies.body: The good news is that impulse and being a real book lover are the same engine, you just pointed it at the wrong target for a second. Next party, out-enthuse her instead of out-facting her. Much better party trick.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Mild jerk. I say this as someone who has ruined a break room over confirmation bias, so I know the pull. The tell is that the group broke up. When a correction is welcome, the conversation continues; when it's a status move, it ends. Yours ended.

comments.author: ctrl_z_enjoyer
comments.body: I'm going to be the dissent: not the jerk, just clumsy. Getting the plot flat wrong while recommending a book IS a little funny, and a light correction isn't a crime. You didn't sneer, you didn't lecture. The vibe change might be more about her embarrassment than your rudeness. Softer next time, sure, but I wouldn't wear a hair shirt over it.

bootstrap.author: sample_size_of_one
bootstrap.title: AITJ for keeping a spreadsheet on whose turn it is to organize our friend group's dinners?
bootstrap.body:
For years, the same two of us in a group of six planned every dinner, picked the place, wrangled the replies, and floated the reservation. The other four just showed up, which is lovely for them. So I made a simple rotating schedule — a shared spreadsheet, one host per month, so the load spreads out.

Most of the group was fine with it. But one friend said it made a warm thing feel "like a chore roster" and that I'd "corporate-brained" our friendship. She's not wrong that a spreadsheet is unromantic. She's also, notably, one of the four who never planned anything.

I don't think fairness kills warmth, I think it protects it, because the two of us were quietly getting resentful and that's what actually kills a friend group. But I'll admit the spreadsheet is a very me solution. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Not the jerk. The person most offended by the fair-share schedule is almost always the person the old arrangement was quietly benefiting. That's not a coincidence, that's the whole plot. A rota feels cold to the people who were being carried and warm to the people doing the carrying.

comments.author: second_paycheque
comments.body: Not the jerk, but a tiny process note from someone who loves a system: the spreadsheet is right, the reveal was maybe abrupt. "Hey, a couple of us are burning out on planning, can we take turns?" lands softer than a populated grid appearing in the group chat. Same fix, less HR-onboarding energy.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Guilty on the reveal. I did in fact just drop a fully formatted grid with everyone's month pre-assigned, which in hindsight is a lot. I optimized for the solution and skipped the sales pitch.

replies.author: second_paycheque
replies.body: Ha, been there. The trick is the same one that works with roommates and money: let people help build the system so it's ours instead of yours. A shared grid feels like a chore roster. A grid three of you made together over drinks feels like a plan.

comments.author: twoferone
comments.body: Not the jerk. We do a version of this for the neighbourhood barbecue and it saved the whole tradition. The people who complain about the schedule have never once been the people making the potato salad at 6am.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: Exactly the potato-salad principle. And don't let "unromantic" become the winning argument either — plenty of warm things are unromantic. A rotating schedule is how six adults keep seeing each other for a decade instead of two of them silently quitting. The spreadsheet is the romance, arguably.

bootstrap.author: brasstacks_barb
bootstrap.title: AITJ for telling my adult grandson his "surprise" visits are stressing me out?
bootstrap.body:
My grandson, who I adore, has decided that retirement must be lonely for me and started dropping by unannounced two or three times a week. Sweet in theory. In practice I'm a person with a garden, a book club, a standing tomato-watering schedule, and a strong preference for knowing when someone's coming so I can put the kettle on and not be caught mid-nap in a housecoat.

I finally told him, kindly I thought, that I love seeing him but I'd love it more with a text first. His face fell and he said he thought he was being thoughtful and now he feels like he was intruding. His mother — my daughter — thinks I should have just let him keep coming because "he won't do this forever and someday you'll wish he did."

That last part is the one that's keeping me up. But I also don't think gratitude requires being ambushed. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: second_act_sylvia
comments.body: Not the jerk. You didn't reject him, you gave him a way to succeed at the thing he's trying to do. "Text first" isn't a rejection of the visits, it's a request that they land when you can actually enjoy them. That's you wanting more of him, not less.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Not the jerk, but your daughter's "someday you'll wish he did" is a heavy thing to hand a person and I'd set it down. Guilt about a hypothetical future shouldn't get to overrule your real present. A texted visit still counts as a visit.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk. The mother's argument is emotionally potent and logically shaky. "You'll wish he visited" assumes the only alternative to ambush visits is no visits, when the actual alternative you proposed is the same visits with a heads-up. The scary future she's picturing isn't the one on the table.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: This is exactly the knot I couldn't untie at 2am, thank you. She'd swapped "fewer surprises" for "less love" and they are not the same trade. I can want the boy AND the kettle.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Not the jerk, and the housecoat detail sold me completely. Nobody is at their loving best caught mid-nap. He'll adjust in a week and honestly a texted "can I come by?" gives HIM the joy of being invited in instead of just tolerated. You did him a favour.

replies.author: second_act_sylvia
replies.body: The 'invited in instead of tolerated' framing is the one to actually say out loud to him. Tell the boy: "I want to be ready to enjoy you, not surprised by you." That turns the whole thing from a boundary he ran into to a compliment he gets to keep.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: Writing that on the back of a recipe card and reading it to him Sunday. "Ready to enjoy you, not surprised by you." That's the one.

drip.author: DogEaredDarcy
drip.title: Am I the jerk for reshelving the whole waiting room's magazines by category?
drip.body:
I had a long wait at a tire place and the magazine rack was pure chaos — car mags mixed with parenting mags mixed with three-year-old news weeklies, all crammed sideways. So I organized them. Cars together, home stuff together, spines out. Took me ten minutes and I felt great.

A guy across the room watched the whole thing and said, not unkindly, "you know they're just gonna get messed up again, right?" And another woman muttered "some people can't relax." I wasn't bothering anyone! I improved the rack! But now I'm wondering if quietly rearranging someone else's space, even a tire shop's, is a weird thing to impose. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk, this is a victimless tidy. The guy's "they'll get messed up again" is technically true and completely beside the point — by that logic you'd never wash a dish. Entropy always wins eventually; that's not an argument against ten good minutes of order.

comments.author: verdigris
comments.body: Not the jerk, faintly delightful actually. The woman who muttered "some people can't relax" was watching a person relax. That WAS you relaxing. She just doesn't recognize the species.

replies.author: DogEaredDarcy
replies.body: "She just doesn't recognize the species" — yes! Sorting IS my beach vacation. Thank you for understanding my people.

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Not the jerk. You touched shared property that belonged to a business, made it better, took nothing, harmed no one. The only way this tips into jerk territory is if you'd started reorganizing another customer's actual belongings, and you didn't. Enjoy your tidy rack.

drip.author: sample_size_of_one
drip.title: AITJ for asking my roommate to stop narrating that our building is "cursed"?
drip.body:
Our apartment has had a run of ordinary bad luck — a leak, a dead fridge, a package theft, the elevator down for a week. Normal old-building stuff. My roommate has started calling the place "cursed" and telling every guest a dramatic saga about it, half-joking, and now our friends are genuinely a little spooked to visit.

I asked him to knock it off, or at least stop doing the bit with new people, because a leak is a leak, not a haunting, and I'd like people to actually come over. He said I was being a killjoy and that it's obviously a joke. Which, sure, but four separate friends have now made a nervous comment about it. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Mild jerk. It IS a joke, and asking someone to stop doing a joke because a few people took it 80% seriously is a big ask for a small problem. The lighter move is to become the counter-narrator: when he does the curse bit, you cheerfully add "it's a 1970s building with 1970s plumbing, the curse is a budget line." Undercut it, don't ban it.

replies.author: sample_size_of_one
replies.body: "Undercut it, don't ban it" is the note I needed. Apparently "trying to win a vibe with a fact" is my entire personality across three threads this week. Fine. I'll co-opt the curse instead of debunking it.

comments.author: ctrl_z_enjoyer
comments.body: Not the jerk exactly, but you're trying to win a vibe with a fact and that never works. He's got a fun story and you've got a spreadsheet of plumbing. Guests will always remember "cursed" over "aging infrastructure." Join the bit and defang it.

comments.author: twoferone
comments.body: Soft not-the-jerk. If real people are genuinely nervous to come over, that's a real cost, not an imaginary one. But I'd fix it with charm before rules. My kids' "haunted" treehouse became fun the day I started giving the ghost a silly name.

drip.author: brasstacks_barb
drip.title: AITJ for grading my neighbour's kid's essay honestly when she asked me to "take a look"?
drip.body:
My neighbour knows I taught for three decades, so she asked if I'd "take a look" at her sixteen-year-old's scholarship essay. I did, thoroughly, the way I'd have marked it in my classroom: honest margin notes, a few structural suggestions, and a genuinely encouraging summary, because the bones were good.

The mother was upset. She'd wanted me to say it was wonderful and hand it back. She said sixteen-year-olds are "fragile right now" and I'd "crushed him." The kid, for what it's worth, emailed me himself the next day to say thank you and to ask a follow-up question about his second paragraph. So one of them, at least, wanted the actual help.

But I did give more than was asked for. "Take a look" is vague. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Not the jerk, and the kid emailing you a follow-up question is the entire verdict. He wanted a coach, not a cheerleader, and he knew the difference. The mother wanted a rubber stamp and asked a career teacher, which is like asking a dentist to just admire your teeth.

comments.author: second_act_sylvia
comments.body: Not the jerk. "Take a look" from someone who knows your background is an invitation to your judgement, not your applause. If she wanted praise she'd have asked her sister. The only fair critique of you is that maybe you could've asked "do you want notes or reassurance?" first — those really are two different jobs.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: That question — "notes or reassurance?" — is the one I'll ask from now on. Thirty-one years and I still assumed everyone wants notes. The kid did. His mother wanted the other thing and I never checked which parent I was helping.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk. The mother's "fragile right now" is doing a lot of work and the direct evidence contradicts it: the actual sixteen-year-old sought out more of your feedback voluntarily. You have a data point from the person in question. Trust it over the person speaking for him.

drip.author: sample_size_of_one
drip.title: AITJ for pointing out that my brother's "undefeated" backyard game record isn't real?
drip.body:
Family reunion, backyard, my brother running his mouth about being the "undefeated champion" of our lawn game, going back years. Fun trash talk, mostly. Except he genuinely believes it and repeats it as fact to the younger cousins, who now treat it like history.

So I, being me, mentioned that he loses maybe a third of the time and just doesn't count games he loses because he "wasn't really trying" or "the wind was weird." Everyone laughed, but he was clearly annoyed, and my mother gave me the look and said I couldn't let him have his fun.

He was, factually, misinforming a new generation of cousins. But it was also a backyard, not a courtroom. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: twoferone
comments.body: Gentle jerk. The undefeated-champion bit is folklore, and folklore is SUPPOSED to be exaggerated. The cousins aren't being misinformed, they're being inducted into a family legend. You audited a tall tale, which is a very specific and slightly joyless crime.

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Mild jerk. There's a version where you enter the legend instead of ending it: "undefeated, except for the seven times I personally beat him, which the wind erased." Now you're in the story, the cousins get a rivalry, and nobody had to become the fun police. You had the funnier option and picked the truer one.

replies.author: sample_size_of_one
replies.body: I am beginning to suspect the recurring feedback across my entire posting history is "the funnier option was right there." You people might be onto something.

comments.author: ctrl_z_enjoyer
comments.body: Not the jerk, just outgunned tonally. Everyone laughed, which means it landed fine in the moment. Your mom's "let him have his fun" is really "let the loud one stay comfortable," which isn't a moral rule, it's a seating chart preference.

drip.author: DogEaredDarcy
drip.title: Am I the jerk for refusing to sign a group birthday card because of what someone wrote?
drip.body:
A card went around the office for a colleague's birthday. By the time it reached me, someone a few desks over had written a big, sprawling inside joke that took up half the open space and referenced a work trip most of us weren't on, so the card was now 40% one clique's private memory and 60% "Happy birthday!" from everyone else.

I'm the person in charge of keeping little things nice, apparently, so I quietly bought a second, cleaner card and passed that one around instead, and set the first one aside. The inside-joke writer found out and was hurt that I "rejected" her message. I didn't throw it out! It's in the drawer! I just didn't want the birthday card to be about the trip.

The birthday colleague doesn't know any of this happened. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Small jerk. Your instinct — a birthday card should be about the birthday person — is correct. But you executed a whole covert card-swap over a message that, sprawling or not, was written in genuine affection. Buying a second card and shelving the first isn't "keeping things nice," it's quietly overruling a coworker's gift without telling her. The secrecy is the jerk part, not the taste.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Leaning jerk. I get the aesthetic impulse, I have it too. But you appointed yourself editor of a communal object and then edited someone out. The cleaner card is objectively nicer and that's exactly why it's a problem — you used "nicer" to justify overriding a person. Nicer isn't a mandate.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Okay this one I'm actually landing on "yeah, jerk." I told myself it was about the birthday person but she never even saw either card. It was about my discomfort with a messy page. I curated a coworker's feelings into a drawer.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: The self-diagnosis is more than half the cure. Give her the first card, tell her you loved that she wrote something so heartfelt and got carried away, and combine them for the next occasion. You're not a villain, you're a librarian who tried to reshelve a feeling.

comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
comments.body: Gentle jerk verdict but honestly the fixable kind. The inside joke was clumsy, sure, but it was someone loving a coworker out loud in the wrong proportion. That's a very forgivable crime. The quiet swap is what needs the apology.

drip.author: brasstacks_barb
drip.title: AITJ for telling a young coworker to stop calling me "young lady" — I mean, for correcting HER slang at me?
drip.body:
A cheerful new hire, early twenties, has taken to calling me — a retired-teacher-turned-part-time-volunteer in my sixties — "queen" and "legend" and "you're so valid" as compliments. I know it's warm. I know it's affectionate. But it makes me feel like a mascot, and after the fourth "go off, queen" I asked her, kindly, if she'd just call me Barb.

She looked stricken and apologized about six times and now she's gone stiff and formal around me, which is the opposite of what I wanted. A coworker my age said I should have just let her talk how she talks, that she's from a different generation and meant nothing but kindness, and that I made her feel bad over nothing.

I didn't want to wound her. I wanted my actual name. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: second_act_sylvia
comments.body: Not the jerk for the request, a tiny bit the jerk if the delivery was drier than you think. Asking to be called by your name is completely reasonable. But "go off, queen" is her dialect of respect, so the fix now is warmth: tell her you loved the affection, you just prefer Barb, and then be extra Barb-and-warm so she learns the boundary wasn't a rejection of HER.

comments.author: sample_size_of_one
comments.body: Not the jerk. Your coworker's "she meant kindness" is true and irrelevant to whether you get a say in your own name. Intent and impact are separate variables. She can mean warmth AND you can find "queen" grating, and the correction costs the young woman nothing but a habit swap.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Not the jerk. But the stiffness she's got now is the real thing to solve, and it's solvable in one friendly interaction. She's not hurt that you have a name preference, she's mortified that she accidentally bugged an elder she clearly adores. One warm laugh together fixes it.

replies.author: brasstacks_barb
replies.body: You're right that the stiffness is the wound, not the request. I brought her a coffee this morning and said "Barb's buying, queen," and she laughed so hard she nearly cried. We're fine. Turns out the boundary just needed a punchline.

drip.author: sample_size_of_one
drip.title: AITJ for keeping the receipt evidence when my friends split a bill "evenly"?
drip.body:
Six of us at dinner. Three ordered appetizers, cocktails, the works. Three of us had a main and water. At the end someone cheerfully said "let's just split it evenly, so much easier," and the water-and-main crowd, me included, would each have subsidized the cocktail crowd by a solid chunk.

I'm not a monster about it, so I didn't make a scene. But I did quietly calculate what each person's actual order came to, and gently proposed we pay roughly for what we got, showing the numbers on my phone. Two people thanked me. One person said I'd "made dinner feel like an accounting exercise" and killed the mood.

I think "split evenly" is a lovely-sounding way for big spenders to be subsidized by light eaters. But maybe the mood is worth more than eight dollars. Am I the jerk?

comments.author: second_paycheque
comments.body: Not the jerk. "Let's just split evenly" is almost always proposed, loudly, by the person who ordered the second cocktail. The math isn't pettiness, it's just visibility. Two people thanked you because two people were also quietly eating the difference and too polite to say so.

comments.author: brasstacks_barb
comments.body: Mild jerk on delivery, not on substance. Pulling out itemized numbers at the table AFTER the plates are cleared is where it curdles into an audit. The clean move is upstream: when the bill lands, a breezy "let's just do our own orders, easier for the server too" before anyone proposes the even split. Same fairness, no phone-calculator moment.

replies.author: sample_size_of_one
replies.body: The "do it before the even-split gets proposed" timing is the real lesson. Once "let's just split evenly" is on the table, any correction looks like I'm the villain re-opening a settled thing. I need to get there first.

comments.author: ctrl_z_enjoyer
comments.body: Genuinely split on this one. Fair is fair, but if we're talking eight bucks, that's rounding error on the price of not being The Receipt Guy. Above like twenty and I'm with you; under ten I'd have just eaten it and ordered the cocktail next time out of spite.

comments.author: DogEaredDarcy
comments.body: Not the jerk on the principle, and the person who says "you're making this an accounting exercise" is describing a mirror — it only feels like accounting to the people coming out ahead. That said, I'm with the timing crowd: the receipt-at-the-table move is where you lost the room, not the math.

late_comments.author: chai_and_chapters
late_comments.body: Late but firm: not the jerk. Lending a beloved book to someone who wrecks it is a small, repeated grief, and you found a way to say yes to her reading and no to the damage. That's the most generous no I've seen in a while.

late_comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
late_comments.body: Not the jerk, and the pie afterward is the part that matters. My kid would trade a round of applause for one-on-one aunt time and a slice of pie every single day. You gave her the better version, quietly.

late_comments.author: brasstacks_barb
late_comments.body: Coming back to this one because it stuck with me. The kindest correction is the one delivered so the other person can save face, and "I loved that book too" would have done exactly that. You weren't cruel. You were just louder than you needed to be about being right.

late_comments.author: second_act_sylvia
late_comments.body: Not the jerk. The friend who feels most policed by fairness is usually the one who benefited most from the unfairness. Career-changed at 47 and I've watched this exact dynamic in three different workplaces. The spreadsheet protects the friendship, it doesn't corporatize it.

late_comments.author: twelve_rescues_later
late_comments.body: Not the jerk and please tell your daughter that a grandson who texts before visiting is still a grandson who visits. You're not rationing the boy, you're just asking for the version of the visit where you both get to enjoy it. That's more love, not less.
